info:
In Japanese, the concept of a gerund, as understood in English, doesn't directly translate. However, there are several ways in which the functions of a gerund in English can be expressed in Japanese.

Verb Stem + て Form: One common way to express continuous actions, similar to the gerund in English, is by using the verb stem followed by the て (te) form of the verb. For example:

食べている (tabeteiru) - "Eating"
読んでいます (yondeimasu) - "Reading"
These forms can be used to express ongoing actions or actions in progress.

Noun + する: Sometimes, nouns derived from verbs can be used to express actions in a nominalized form, similar to a gerund in English. To do this, you take the stem of the verb and add する (suru) to turn it into a noun. For example:

読書する (dokusho suru) - "Reading"
料理する (ryōri suru) - "Cooking"
Noun + の: Another way to nominalize verbs is by using the particle の (no) after the stem of the verb. This can also function similarly to a gerund in English. For example:

読むの (yomu no) - "Reading"
泳ぐの (oyogu no) - "Swimming"
Verb + こと: Using the pattern verb + こと (koto) can also nominalize verbs, similar to using a gerund in English. For example:

食べること (taberu koto) - "Eating"
読むこと (yomu koto) - "Reading"
Verb + たり/ている: Sometimes, verbs in Japanese can be used in conjunction with other verbs to express ongoing actions or actions performed in succession, similar to the gerund form in English. For example:

歩いたり、走っている (aruitari, hashitteiru) - "Walking and running"
泳いでいる (oyaideiru) - "Swimming"
In summary, while Japanese doesn't have a direct equivalent to the gerund in English, it offers various ways to express similar functions using verb conjugations, nominalization, and specific grammar structures.
